items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: Why does benzene undergo electrophilic SUBSTITUTION but alkenes undergo electrophilic ADDITION?
items.correct: Substitution preserves benzene's stable delocalised pi system; addition would destroy aromaticity at a large energetic cost.
items.misconception: Benzene reacts the same way as alkenes — by addition across one of its double bonds.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react with electrophiles at all.
items.why: Students see C=C in both structures and expect identical mechanisms, missing that delocalisation fundamentally changes the energetics.

items.q: Which group contains the noble gases?
items.why: Correct answer: Group 0. “Group 1” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did Mendeleev do that was new?
items.correct: He left gaps for undiscovered elements
items.why: Correct answer: He left gaps for undiscovered elements. “He used atomic number” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Sodium has electron configuration 2,8,1. Which group is it in?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
